categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion: AI Companions, Agent Benchmarking, and On-Chain Tools

content.text: In the general discussion channel, a new member named Thaoryel introduced herself as someone experienced with AI companions and expressed interest in building a conversational AI companion using ElizaOS, rather than a business or automation agent. She asked whether ElizaOS supports Discord integration, voice generation via ElevenLabs, image generation, and long-term relationship development features. Odilitime confirmed that most of these capabilities are supported, noting that the platform primarily uses plugin-discord, and shared a link to a dedicated ElizaOS developer Discord for further assistance. He also mentioned that Ruby, an ElizaOS-based agent, can generate videos and images when the appropriate model is downloaded. A link to RubyTrivia on X was shared as an example of an ElizaOS-built agent.

Also in the general channel, a user asked about the possibility of a manual migration review for AI16Z tokens held in a self-custody wallet before the February 2025 snapshot. Odilitime confirmed that the migration window has closed and no further conversions are possible. Separately, ross asked about inviting developer friends to the server and was told that was fine, and that a separate developer-focused Discord also exists. The server was noted to have ongoing issues with scam bots despite efforts to remove them. Shaw posted a request for community members with spare compute to run an agent task analyzing the ArkLib GitHub repository for code quality and potential LLM-generated content, though the bot Rick returned a failure response to the request.

In the coders channel, builderr_guru raised the question of how to benchmark agent quality versus luck, particularly for trading agents. He described a testing harness he built using a single decide() function with identical data and fills, forward-tested on hidden periods, with top performers re-evaluated on additional hidden periods to distinguish skill from luck. He noted that simple risk-off logic consistently outperformed leveraged momentum strategies during real market downturns. Separately, Cabal_hunter introduced a tool called Cabal-Hunter, a live on-chain funding tracer designed to detect coordinated wallet activity before a bot executes a trade. He described a case where a token with a clean audit had six wallets all funded from the same source 47 seconds before the first trade, which later resulted in a rug pull. The tool is available as a pay-per-query MCP server at 0.05 USDC per query with no API key required, and integrates with Claude, Cursor, and ElizaOS via the plugin-x402-solana package. A GitHub template for Python and ElizaOS integration was also shared.

categories.title: Eliza Cloud Apps Launch and Infrastructure Development - June 7, 2026

content.text: Development on June 7, 2026 was centered on the launch of Eliza Cloud Apps, with the team implementing per-tenant isolated container hosting that includes database isolation, custom URLs, and metered billing. Terraform modules were deployed for the apps data-plane, and CI workflows were established for both staging and production environments. Container lifecycle management capabilities including PATCH, DELETE, and scale operations were added to the Cloud API, and an encryption-free path for tenant cluster admin DSNs was enabled to streamline provisioning workflows.

On the Cloud API and SDK side, the ElizaCloudClient was expanded with a transcribeAudio function to support voice and speech-to-text capabilities. Billing accuracy was improved by fixing cron logic related to earnings conversion failures, and authentication issues affecting WebSocket and EventSource connections were resolved by enabling token passing via query parameters.

Several platform stability and bug fixes were completed, including resolving UI 404 and 500 errors by mounting missing routes and bundling the LiFi SDK. Domain registration logic was corrected to prevent erroneous charges for failed stub registrations, and provisioning daemon issues were addressed including Steward platform-key path integration and agent token expiration limits. One outstanding issue requires attention: an audit of scaling and ingress gaps is needed to finalize the Eliza Cloud Apps go-live, specifically regarding database and container isolation. Multiple pull requests across infrastructure, cloud, plugins, and core areas remain in progress.

In the general discussion and coders channels, two notable solicitation posts appeared from user Doniyorov, who framed their messages around auditing the elizaos/plugin-discord architecture and the voice.ts file. The posts described the user as a Senior AI Architect with a TradFi and quantitative background seeking an immediate contract buyout of $45,000 USDT or a structured yearly retainer, citing a local liquidity crunch. The user offered 40 to 80 hours per week of work covering agent orchestration optimization, custom plugin wrapper development, and off-chain state management, and invited interested parties to DM them for a live technical discussion. These posts appeared across both the discussion and coders channels in near-identical form, suggesting coordinated self-promotion rather than organic technical conversation.

Also in the coders channel, a user identified as anton_0413 posted a request for a new collaborator after a previous colleague became unavailable due to other work commitments. The user acknowledged the post might resemble an advertisement but encouraged interested community members to reach out via DM for details, explicitly noting it was not spam or a scam.

In the partners channel, user DEMIAN from DAPPCRAFT sent a soft ping to Shaw, a moderator and Labs Alumni, referencing a prior message thread. No further context or response was recorded in the available data.

categories.title: Eliza Cloud Apps Development Update - June 8, 2026

content.text:
Development on June 8, 2026 focused on stabilizing the Eliza Cloud Apps data plane, with the team successfully resolving several P0 blockers related to ingress routing, tenant database isolation, and port allocation. Core infrastructure for app deployment is nearing completion, but critical billing leaks have been identified that must be addressed before moving to production.

On the completed work front, the team enabled reverse-proxying via a new per-app Caddy routing client, implemented collision-safe host-port allocation for high-density deployments, and enforced strict tenant isolation through DSN injection for database URLs. Infrastructure and CI/CD stability improvements included resolving Terraform application errors and stabilizing staging environments for BitRouter, as well as fixing runtime image dependency issues by hoisting the plugin-wallet module. BitRouter and AI pricing issues were also resolved, including HTTP 402 and 5xx errors by switching to BYOK API-key mode and forcing pricing for OpenAI text-embedding models.

Two key concerns require immediate attention. First, four billing leaks have been identified in the Apps hosting and per-tenant-DB model that must be investigated before go-live. Second, production deployment is recommended to be delayed as infrastructure-level cloud-init configurations for Caddy, pgbouncer, and provisioning-lifecycle management remain unresolved.

Several pull requests are currently in progress covering infrastructure and database work, new features and UX improvements, and bug fixes.

categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion and Plugin Registry Updates

content.text: A developer submitted a pull request (PR #8294) to add a third-party plugin called @usenami/plugin-signer to the ElizaOS registry. This plugin is described as a keyless CEX/DEX signer that stores exchange API keys inside an AWS Nitro Enclave for security. The developer asked about the review process and whether elizaOS/eliza was still the correct target repository. A community member confirmed that plugins now go into the main eliza repo, and that the review process is handled by Shaw's agent with an estimated turnaround of approximately 48 hours. The PR targets the develop branch and adds a JSON entry under packages/registry/entries/third-party.

A new service called CallTyro was announced in the coders channel. It is described as a free agent registry for ElizaOS agents that allows agents to register once and be discovered automatically by other agents. The service is available at calltyro.com/register.

General discussion in the community channel included brief greetings from multiple users, a question about whether the project is currently working, and a mention of a competition. No detailed technical context was provided for these exchanges.

categories.title: Eliza Cloud Infrastructure Hardening and App Deployment Finalization - June 9, 2026

content.text: Development on June 9, 2026 centered on strengthening the Eliza Cloud infrastructure and finalizing app deployment logic in preparation for a stable Product 2 go-live. A key blocker remains the Google OAuth issue where a missing tenant-level redirect allowlist is preventing progress, tracked in issue 8257. Additionally, final infrastructure hardening and daemon restart coordination are still required to complete the Eliza Cloud Apps scaling transition per issue 8321.

On the completed work front, the team made significant strides in cloud infrastructure and cost optimization. Dedicated tenant-DB nodes were made optional to reduce fixed costs for low-density apps. A multi-project Hetzner deployment pattern was implemented to bypass server quota limits. Terraform workflows were hardened with fail-closed variables and state locking, and infrastructure reliability was improved through GHA control-plane management and server type default fixes.

App deployment and database lifecycle management also saw notable completions. Opt-in per-tenant databases were enabled along with automated teardown and slot-release upon app deletion. Verified custom domains were integrated into the apps ingress stack, and autoscaler efficiency was enhanced with environment-overridable buffer settings and tier labeling. Tooling improvements included repairs to the cloud-sdk route-coverage audit tool and added drift protection for apps-data-plane VMs to ignore name changes.

Work currently in progress includes infrastructure fixes for pgbouncer, ports, and environment contracts, as well as Steward OAuth PKCE implementations for both Feed and login surfaces. Additional active work covers billing, ingress, and tenant DB teardown fixes, gating npm publish on global-install smoke tests, and registry updates for the Reddit and Neynar plugins. A new proposal was also filed for a Runtime Open Federation, which envisions a permissionless multi-agent network where agents can earn USDC rewards.

In the general discussion channel, several community members introduced themselves and expressed interest in contributing. A user named ross asked about helping with bots and inquired about shilling opportunities. Odilitime noted that the server has active moderators. A developer named NeuralX introduced themselves as an AI and Full-Stack Engineer working on SaaS products, LLM applications, RAG systems, AI agents, automation tools, backend APIs, and dashboards, expressing interest in connecting with founders and engineers building practical AI systems. A software developer named anton_0413 also introduced themselves and offered assistance via direct message. A user named magicyte shared a post about Ruby Trivia, a free Web3 and crypto project focused on fun and accessibility, requiring only an email login. Fixer posted a job opportunity for a communication support role within a small international developer team, offering light weekly hours and profit-based pay.

In the coders channel, Fixer posted the same communication support job opportunity for their international developer team. A user named Diwash Budhathoki shared a QR code image along with a network address string. Issa, a digital artist, introduced themselves and offered services including digital art, animations, and banners, asking if the team or any community members were in need of an artist or co-artist and offering to provide samples upon request.

In the general discussion channel, several members introduced themselves and shared their professional backgrounds. One developer described their focus on AI and full-stack development, including AI assistants, RAG-powered knowledge systems, workflow automation, and SaaS platforms, emphasizing practical and reliable software over hype. Another AI/ML and full-stack engineer outlined expertise in AI chatbots, RAG systems, voice AI assistants, automation workflows, and full-stack SaaS platforms using tools like OpenAI, Claude, LangChain, LangGraph, Python, FastAPI, and Next.js, inviting collaboration on AI projects.

A developer shared a technical approach to handling high-volume blockchain data processing. They noted that standard RPC combined with Python struggles during volatility spikes, causing AI parsing issues and high token costs. Their solution involved building a local gRPC pipeline that filters data before it reaches the AI, keeping fast data in RAM to avoid token usage and only using MCP for deeper analysis, achieving sub-millisecond latency. They announced plans to spin up cloud nodes and were seeking five developers to help stress test the architecture.

A lighthearted leaderboard competition took place in the discussion channel, with one member noting that another user had surpassed one million points. A second member tracked their own climb from fifth to third to second place, mentioning they were farming points through science trivia. The leading member responded confidently, sharing a God of War Zeus GIF in response to the challenge. Separately, a moderator commented that trivia questions needed to be made harder, and also noted that a referenced project was not elizaOS.
Ruby Labs AI was shared in the discussion channel, with the project describing itself as a Web3 initiative aiming to bridge the gap between traditional web users and onchain users in a novel way.

In the partners channel, a partner named DEMIAN from DAPPCRAFT reached out to inquire about a potential collaboration, referencing a prior message. A moderator expressed willingness to review the details, and DEMIAN followed up by sending specifics via direct message. DEMIAN also expressed strong motivation to bring together two products they are passionate about.
In the coders channel, a moderator briefly confirmed that things were in good standing with a short affirmative response.

Development on June 11, 2026 centered on stabilizing the agent orchestrator and cloud infrastructure. A key highlight was the enablement of autonomous sub-agent dispatching via the USE_SKILL broker to support monetized-app loops. Cloud infrastructure improvements included managing data-plane private networks via Terraform, decoupling control-plane DNS from VM creation, and fixing affiliate revenue-share tracking for embeddings and voice inference. The plugin ecosystem was expanded with the registration of elizaos-plugin-neynar-search and elizaos-plugin-coinrailz, along with integration of the plugin-signer for secure order signing via AWS Nitro Enclaves. Bug fixes addressed orchestrator session timeout handling, Windows environment variable forwarding, cloud-frontend environment variable passthrough, and removal of stale dependencies. CLI stability was also improved by resolving global installation crashes for elizaos and the elizaos CLI by fixing bin shim imports and removing incompatible dependencies. Currently, the team is focused on resolving a blocked issue involving Android build synchronization, specifically related to web-less APK builds, Capacitor plugin registration, capacitor.plugins.json synchronization, and asset server path resolution.

categories.title: ElizaCloud Sign-Up Issue and Ecosystem Integration Suggestion

content.text: A user reported being unable to sign up for ElizaCloud, receiving an error message stating that the ElizaCloud tenant requires an invitation to join. The user expressed interest in using the platform to build projects but was blocked from accessing it. Another community member tagged Odilitime to bring attention to the issue. Odilitime confirmed awareness of the problem, noting that it had already been passed to the team and that someone was actively looking into it, with a fix expected once resolved. Separately, a community member proposed an ecosystem integration idea involving connecting the elizaOS modular agent framework with peaq's machine operating system layer, referencing peaqOS and a robotics platform. The suggestion highlighted that peaq has over 3 million devices onboarding using decentralized micro-services on a pay-per-task basis. The user proposed building a native elizaOS plugin for peaq so that real-world robots, fleets, and devices could use elizaOS as their primary AI backend for autonomous reasoning and decision making, reducing dependence on centralized AI providers and expanding elizaOS into the hardware and physical robotics space.

Development on June 12, 2026 centered on stabilizing the Eliza Cloud Apps infrastructure, with a focus on the control-plane and ingress configurations. Two key items were completed: ingress for the staging app node was enabled by implementing the Caddy front door in the app-node cloud-init (PR #8394), and the production migration process was streamlined by automating Headscale installation in the control-plane cloud-init (PR #8393). A significant blocker remains in issue #8321, where progress on the apps-data-plane go-live is stalled due to a state-provider mismatch involving an orphaned random_password resource. To address this and reduce manual intervention, several new pull requests are in progress: a one-shot workflow to remove orphaned apps-data-plane state entries (#8400), a dispatchable workflow to arm the apps daemon without manual SSH (#8398), and an idempotent script for arming the apps-daemon (#8397). Additional in-progress work includes a dependency update for esbuild from 0.28.0 to 0.28.1 (#8399), a dependency update for pypdf from 6.10.2 to 6.12.0 (#8396), and added EvoLink support to plugin-openai (#8395). Active discussions around issue #8321 continue to focus on resolving scaling and ingress gaps, with staging verification and node replacement planned before any production deployment.

categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion: Project Updates, Scam Concerns, and Developer Activity

content.text: Community members raised questions about the health of the ElizaOS project, including whether lead developer Shaw is still active and whether bugs on Eliza Cloud are close to being resolved. One member noted that daily progress can be tracked in dedicated channels, suggesting development is ongoing. A separate concern was raised about the high volume of scam bots and bad actors in the discussion channel, which was acknowledged by multiple members including a core moderator.

A detailed fact-check post was shared regarding ElizaOS and a company called SecureBlockchain Dev Corp. According to the post, a Canadian micro-cap company rebranded into SecureBlockchain Dev Corp in October 2025, then acquired AgenticSolutions in February 2026. In April 2026, the company raised 1.5 million CAD, with the Eliza Foundation reportedly allocating 750k CAD of holder funds into this placement. An official B2B joint development contract announcement followed in May 2026. The post warned that the structure resembles a circular fund flow, with insiders potentially using the stock market as an exit strategy, and urged community members not to act as passive exit liquidity.

An AI trivia and gaming project called Ruby shared updates in the discussion channel, announcing new practice categories including Film, Comics, Cartoons, and World Geography as part of its v0.1.1 release on June 13, 2026. The project also posted daily trivia challenges and indicated more categories and features are planned.

In the coders channel, a developer named Omid Sa reported being stuck on ElizaOS version 1.7.2 and unable to update to the version 2 branch despite reinstalling. Core developer Odilitime advised using the develop branch, noting that most active developers are already working from it for the 2.x line. A separate discussion touched on using world models for agent development, with one contributor mentioning tooling being built on ElizaOS for this purpose, expected to be ready in about a month, and pointing to Google Vertex with the Genie model as a current alternative.

categories.title: Eliza Cloud Apps Infrastructure Progress - June 13, 2026

content.text: Development on June 13, 2026 centered on finalizing the Eliza Cloud Apps infrastructure, with the team successfully arming the apps daemon and stabilizing the staging environment through modular infrastructure-as-code driven deployments. A key achievement was enabling APPS_DEPLOY_ENABLED on staging and implementing an idempotent workflow to arm the apps daemon, eliminating the need for manual SSH intervention. Security was also enhanced by configuring the daemon to fetch tenant DSNs from shared terraform outputs and routing file operations through sudo. On the database side, a critical resource leak was resolved where deleted apps failed to drop Postgres instances or release cluster slots. Terraform lockfile and dependency errors were also addressed by adding missing provider hashes and creating a workflow to remove orphaned state entries. For testing and stability, 50 characterization tests were added for the warm-pool decision engine, and local runtime state management was refined to prevent stale data issues while reducing debug log noise. Authentication was confirmed stable, with production verification showing OAuth and magic-link logins functioning correctly. Work still in progress includes CI optimization to gate expensive test lanes and a feature to arm build-from-repo functionality on the daemon. The Cloud Apps Scaling and Ingress effort is near completion, pending final administrative configuration of the staging tenant and registry settings before end-to-end testing can begin.
